raw_consumer_statement:
I booked a flight for my wife and XXXX children on XX/XX/year> for {$1700.00} with XXXX XXXX using my American express credit card ending XXXX. I was sent one confirmation email with Booking Reference XXXX. Ticket number of the lead passenger is XXXX. 

Two separate charges for the same amount appeared on my statement, each for {$1700.00} with the same date ( XX/XX/XXXX ). 

I disputed the duplicate charge with XXXX XXXX who initially said I had been charged once. I then disputed the charge with Amex multiple times. Each time, the Amex agent I spoke to agreed there had been a mistake and filed a dispute with the " back office '' team. However, the three official responses from XXXX XXXX and this team the did not resolve the dispute. 

The first official response ( XX/XX/XXXX ) provides a refund of {$450.00}. It is not clear why this particular amount was refunded rather than the full amount. 

The second official response ( XX/XX/XXXX ) declines further refund of the remaining {$1300.00}. The second response is accompanied by information from XXXX XXXX on the disputed, duplicate charge. The Booking Reference ( " Confirmation ( PNR ) '' ) is XXXX. Ticket number for the lead passenger is XXXX. I have no record of any booking or confirmation email with this reference or ticket number. The document contains " proof of payment '' ( page 16 ) but this is not evidence that I initiated or approved the transaction ( which I did not ), just the details of the payment they took from Amex. 

The third official response ( XX/XX/XXXX ) is inconsistent and confusing. It acknowledges two separate transactions and claims that the disputed transaction ( ticket number : XXXX ) was paid for on a card ending XXXX. I do not have a credit card ending in these numbers. In contrast, the supporting evidence from XXXX XXXX for this transaction states : " Merchant response type : Accept '' and " The merchant accepts this dispute. No rebuttal documents to be sent. '' But it also states : " No credits to be issued. 

Aside from just messing up on the third response, a key issue here is that Amex does not seem to have a robust process for determining if a customer initiated or approved an internet transaction in the case of duplicated charges ( where, by definition, the merchant has valid payment details ). The third response contains details of the valid transaction ( reference XXXX, ticket : XXXX ) including the IP address used. These details are missing from the disputed charge. The the lack of these details indicates that XXXX initiated the disputed transaction, not me. Amex could have deduced that from their omission ( but probably needs an even more robust process ).
